claim: Large context windows do not fix agent attention; models exhibit a U-curve pattern where they focus on initial and final context but drop middle sections.
actionable: Don't assume bigger context windows solve agent problems; instead use context optimization techniques like hierarchical summarization, iterative retrieval, or self-correction to ensure relevant context is attended to.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: Context optimization techniques include hierarchical summarization (summaries per file), knowledge graphs (for logical dependencies), and iterative retrieval (indexed topic lookup).
actionable: Choose a context optimization strategy: use hierarchical summarization for simple tasks, knowledge graphs for complex inter-file dependencies, or iterative retrieval for most general cases.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: The orchestration paradox: capable models spend tokens on deciding how to solve problems rather than solving them, wasting API costs on planning loops.
actionable: To avoid endless planning loops, split tasks into 80% research (high-reasoning models exploring options) and 20% validation (lighter models confirming results), with hard stopping conditions.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: An 80/20 split assigns high-reasoning models to open-ended discovery and lighter models to deterministic validation, reducing wasted reasoning tokens.
actionable: Use an expensive high-reasoning model for planning and discovery, then route to a cheaper lightweight model for validation, summarization, and deterministic gates.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: Multi-agent architectures with a judge node help prevent overwhelmed agents from losing sight of the original task when given too many concurrent goals.
actionable: When an agent needs to handle multiple tasks (review, security, testing), split into specialist agents, then use a judge agent to recombine results and weight them against historical PR patterns.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: Judge agents can reference PR history to weight and rank recommendations, learning which types of suggestions are accepted and adjusting confidence scores over time.
actionable: In multi-agent systems, have the judge agent inspect historical PR data to understand which similar issues were accepted vs rejected, then weight current recommendations accordingly.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: Context engines require significant upfront indexing and mapping effort; hierarchical summarization and iterative retrieval are lower-effort alternatives for most tasks.
actionable: Before building a context engine, try iterative retrieval or hierarchical summarization; save context engines for when you manage hundreds of repos and need sophisticated ranking.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo

claim: Self-correction with a critic node checks if outputs align with goals and allows retries, adding latency but improving accuracy without upfront developer work.
actionable: Add a critic node after agent tasks to evaluate outputs against original goals; if the critic rejects the output, allow the agent to retry with feedback.
source_title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo
